chain_of_thought: Top native frame is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum inside libUbikeyJni.so, called from ASN.1-to-X509 conversion routines and ultimately from CERT_Load, then JNI jGetOrg, then fuzz harness.

Function name strongly suggests it walks ASN.1 structure children and computes a child count or index, which is a classic place for out-of-bounds on malformed ASN.1 sequences/certs. Crash reason text matches function name, so likely memory-safety issue inside this routine (e.g., invalid child index, corrupted node pointer, bad length).

The call stack is entirely within the app’s own crypto/X509 parsing implementation, not in system allocators or harness-only code. There is no indication this is a harness artifact like passing null where app never would; fuzz harness just calls the JNI bridge similarly to real app Java. CERT_Load/Convert_ASN1_to_X509_* imply they parse certificates and then extract organization (subject field) via jGetOrg.

ASN.1/X509 parsing is by design driven by attacker-controlled input: certificates from remote servers, NFC, files, etc. If malformed input causes Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum to crash, that is a realistic trigger path in production, not just fuzz-only. Even if the immediate fuzzer input is synthetic, it models untrusted certificate blobs the app will accept from external sources.

Because we lack binary-level details (Ghidra/Jadx tools unavailable due to 404) we can’t see the exact operation (memcpy vs. array index), nor can we prove presence/absence of bounds checks. However, a dedicated helper named Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum crashing under fuzz almost always indicates misuse of in-structure lengths or child counters, which is a memory safety issue (out-of-bounds read/write, invalid deref) or at least a robust parsing failure on malformed but syntactically possible encodings.

Given realistic attacker control over cert contents, and standard patterns of ASN.1 parser bugs, it is reasonable to treat this as a likely vulnerability. Impact is at least denial-of-service (certificate that crashes app when loaded); depending on the exact corruption, it might be exploitable memory corruption (CWE-787 or similar). Without decompilation we should assign high but not absolute confidence and classify severity as high due to potential memory corruption in native code on attacker input.

We assume: jGetOrg takes a certificate or data directly or indirectly influenced by untrusted sources; no strong validation happens in Java before JNI; and Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum doesn’t fully check structure bounds (as indicated by crash). Limitations: no instruction-level trace, no precise fault type (SIGSEGV vs abort), no direct view of Java call graph arguments. Still, the contextual evidence supports a real, attacker-reachable bug more than an environmental artifact.
reasons: Crash occurs in custom ASN.1/X509 parsing helper Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum within app native library, not in harness or allocator
ASN.1/X509 parsing is driven by attacker-controlled certificate data, so malformed external inputs can reach this function via CERT_Load and JNI jGetOrg
Function purpose (computing child number in ASN.1 tree) is a classic source of out-of-bounds or invalid dereference on malformed structures, consistent with a memory-safety bug
Call stack shows a realistic parsing path (CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → … → jGetOrg), not a synthetic-only harness path
recommendations: Statically and dynamically audit ASN.1/X509 parsing code in libUbikeyJni.so, especially Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and callers, for strict bounds and length checks on all child indices and lengths
Harden CERT_Load/Convert_ASN1_to_X509_* to validate overall ASN.1 structure size, tag, and length fields before recursing/walking children
Add fuzz and negative tests around jGetOrg and certificate loading with malformed ASN.1 inputs; enable ASan/UBSan builds to localize the exact memory error
assumptions: jGetOrg processes certificate data that can ultimately be influenced by remote or otherwise untrusted inputs
The crash is due to memory-safety issues in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum rather than an intentional abort or harness-only misuse
Java layer does not fully sanitize certificate structure before passing into native CERT_Load
limitations: No decompiled code available for Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum or CERT_Load to confirm exact faulty operation
Exact signal/exception type and faulting address are unknown, so precise CWE may differ (e.g., invalid deref vs. OOB read/write)

prerequisites: Attacker can cause the application to load or inspect an externally supplied certificate (e.g., remote TLS endpoint, file import, NFC, or QR-based cert)
libUbikeyJni.so is present and used for certificate parsing in the deployed app version
No upstream sanitization rejects the malformed ASN.1 before Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum is called

Deliver this certificate to the app via a reachable channel (e.g., server certificate in a TLS connection, imported file, or IPC payload)
Trigger the code path that calls UbikeyJni.jGetOrg, ensuring CERT_Load parses the malicious certificate
When the parser walks the malformed ASN.1 tree and computes child indices, it hits the bug in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, causing memory corruption or at least an application crash

chain_of_thought: Tool calls failed with 404, so detailed decompilation is unavailable; must reason from crash metadata alone.
Top native frame is __memcpy, called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST inside libUbikeyJni.so, suggesting a buffer copy during DER ASN.1 parsing of a certificate structure.
Call stack shows Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> CERT_Load -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg; this is clearly app/library logic, not harness-only glue.

Fuzzer entry fuzz_one_input stands in for an arbitrary Java/native caller; in the real app, certificate data would come from external sources (server, smart card, etc.), so attacker-controlled ASN.1 is realistic.

__memcpy crash implies an out-of-bounds read/write or overlap based on an incorrect length; typical DER decoding bugs involve trusting embedded length fields without validating against the overall buffer size.
Even without code, the function name Decode_DER_ASN1_ST strongly indicates handwritten or 3rd-party ASN.1 decoder logic, a common source of memory corruption when lengths are not checked.
Because the crash occurs directly in memcpy during decode, not in an explicit abort/sanitizer frame, this is likely a genuine memory safety issue rather than a deliberate bounds check.

JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg is a public native API used by the app; there is no evidence that it performs strict length validation or constrains inputs beyond what the native certificate loader sees.
Given standard patterns, CERT_Load probably takes a pointer+length to certificate bytes; if fuzzer can trigger memcpy OOB via malformed DER, a malicious remote endpoint or file could too.

Without decompilation we cannot pinpoint exact missing bounds check, but the presence of a memcpy-based crash in ASN.1 decoding is strong circumstantial evidence of CWE-787/CWE-119 style bug, likely exploitable.
Impact: memory corruption in a security-sensitive library (certificate parsing), plausible for RCE or at least process compromise; classify as high severity with high, but not absolute, confidence.

Exploit sketch: attacker supplies malformed certificate (e.g., via TLS handshake, file import, or QR/NFC) that causes decoder to compute excessive length and memcpy beyond buffer bounds, corrupting heap/stack and enabling code execution under app context.
reasons: Crash in __memcpy invoked by Decode_DER_ASN1_ST during certificate ASN.1 decoding, typical of out-of-bounds memory copy
Length/structure of DER input is realistically attacker-controlled via certificates or similar external data
Crash path is pure app/library logic (no harness-only frames) inside libUbikeyJni.so, suggesting a genuine code bug rather than environment misuse
JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg wraps CERT_Load, exposing this path to untrusted inputs
recommendations: Review and harden Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and related ASN.1 parsing code to validate all length fields against the overall buffer size before memcpy/memmove
Add centralized, bounds-checked helper routines for DER length extraction and buffer copying, and replace raw memcpy calls with them
Integrate ASan/Valgrind-style testing on certificate parsing with a corpus of malformed DER inputs to confirm and fix all similar issues
assumptions: Certificate/ASN.1 input to CERT_Load is derived from potentially untrusted external sources in the real app (server, files, tokens)
JNI method Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg does not fully re-validate ASN.1 structure or strictly cap input sizes before calling CERT_Load
The __memcpy crash corresponds to an out-of-bounds access rather than a benign harness-induced abort
limitations: No direct decompilation of libUbikeyJni.so functions was available, so the exact faulty memcpy callsite and bounds check logic could not be inspected
Java-side call graph and concrete sources of certificate data in the app were not analyzed, so exact attacker entrypoints are inferred
Exploitability (e.g., controlled overwrite vs. crash-only) cannot be precisely determined without memory layout and allocator behavior

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a malformed ASN.1/DER-encoded certificate or similar blob that is parsed via UbikeyJni.jGetOrg
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process attacker-supplied certificate or DER-encoded data through the UbikeyJni.jGetOrg path
Native library libUbikeyJni.so is present and used in production builds
No additional sandboxing prevents exploitation of memory corruption in this process
exploit_pipeline: Identify a feature path (e.g., TLS handshake, certificate import, smart card read) that invokes UbikeyJni.jGetOrg and ultimately CERT_Load with attacker-influenced certificate bytes
Craft ASN.1/DER payloads with malformed length fields to cause Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to compute an out-of-range length for memcpy
Iterate using fuzzing and differential testing on a rooted device or emulator to find payloads that produce controlled heap/stack corruption instead of immediate abort
Refine payload to overwrite function pointers or object vtables in a predictable manner, achieving code execution within the app process
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.kbankwith.smartbank/.MainActivity --es cert_path /sdcard/Download/malformed_cert.der

# Or, if triggered via instrumentation test harness:
adb shell am instrument -w -e der_path /sdcard/Download/malformed_cert.der com.kbankwith.smartbank.test/androidx.test.runner.AndroidJUnitRunner
exploit.notes: Exact app component and intent extras are placeholders; replace MainActivity and parameter names with the actual ones that reach UbikeyJni.jGetOrg once identified via static or dynamic analysis.

chain_of_thought: Native stack shows __memcpy -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> CERT_Load -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg -> fuzz_one_input -> main, with __memcpy as the terminating function, suggesting a buffer overrun or invalid pointer use during a memcpy inside DER ASN.1 decoding or certificate loading.

Ghidra MCP calls to decompile Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, CERT_Load, and the JNI function failed with 404 errors, and listing functions also failed, so I cannot inspect the exact implementation or track memcpy arguments (src, dst, len) inside the native library as required by the ideal workflow.

However, semantic naming strongly implies Decode_DER_ASN1_ST parses attacker-provided DER data into internal structures; CERT_Load likely takes a buffer (certificate bytes) and length and invokes Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, where __memcpy is ultimately called. Such parsing functions are classic sources of memory corruption if length fields inside DER (e.g., ASN.1 length fields) are trusted without proper bounds checking.

The JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg is called from Java method UbikeyJni.jGetOrg(byte[]): String; fuzz harness passes arbitrary data to this method, which suggests its first parameter is a byte[] representing certificate or key material under test. In real usage, this would plausibly come from untrusted sources (remote server certificates, files, tokens).

Because the crash occurs directly in __memcpy, and the call path is through ASN.1 / CERT parsing, the most likely root cause is that Decode_DER_ASN1_ST uses a memcpy or equivalent with a length derived from DER-encoded length fields or certificate content without enforcing that the source buffer actually contains that many bytes, or without verifying the destination buffer is large enough.

No evidence suggests this is a harness-only artifact like forced NULL pointers or impossible ownership patterns; instead, the fuzz harness simply stands in for real callers that would pass externally-provided certificate data. The JNI name and Java signature confirm that a raw byte array is passed to native without any obvious enforced size constraints at the JVM boundary.

Given that DER ASN.1 decoding is complex and historically error-prone, and that the crash is a hard memcpy failure rather than a graceful parse error, it is reasonable to classify this as a likely out-of-bounds read/write or invalid-pointer access vulnerability in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or CERT_Load.

Without native code decompilation I cannot precisely determine whether it is a read or write overflow nor the exact taint path, but the combination of function names, crash location, and fuzz-induced crash on arbitrary input fits well with a memory corruption bug exploitable for at least denial-of-service and potentially more, if an attacker can supply crafted certificates or DER blobs.

Exploitability in-the-wild depends on where UbikeyJni.jGetOrg is used—likely within authentication, key management, or certificate handling paths. If this method is invoked on data from a remote server or from NFC tokens/smart cards, a remote or proximity attacker could supply malformed DER to crash the app or potentially leverage heap corruption. Due to the missing detailed code, I keep confidence below 0.9 but above 0.6.

Therefore I classify this crash as a likely genuine vulnerability: an out-of-bounds memory operation in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST/CERT_Load reachable via JNI with attacker-controlled DER/certificate input, leading to memory corruption and app-level DoS, with potential for more severe impact depending on allocator and environment.
reasons: Crash occurs inside __memcpy invoked from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST during certificate/ASN.1 decoding, indicating an out-of-bounds copy or invalid pointer.
JNI method Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetOrg accepts a raw byte[] from Java, which in realistic flows would be derived from untrusted certificate/DER data.
Fuzz harness only replaces normal callers supplying bytes; no signs of harness-only artifacts such as forced NULL, so the memcpy misuse is likely present in production code.
ASN.1/DER parsing is historically vulnerable to length-handling bugs, and a hard memcpy crash strongly suggests missing bounds validation on attacker-influenced lengths or offsets.
recommendations: Reverse engineer Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load to locate memcpy/memmove calls and enforce strict bounds checks on all ASN.1-derived lengths before copying.
Add robust DER/ASN.1 validation that ensures declared lengths do not exceed the input buffer size or destination structure capacity, and return parse errors instead of copying blindly.
Augment testing with targeted ASN.1/DER fuzzing (e.g., afl, libFuzzer, honggfuzz) around UbikeyJni.jGetOrg and certificate handling code, compiled with ASan/UBSan if possible.
assumptions: UbikeyJni.jGetOrg(byte[]) forwards the input byte array more or less directly to CERT_Load/Decode_DER_ASN1_ST without fully constraining sizes.
The byte[] parameter in real deployments is populated from potentially untrusted sources such as remote server certificates or user-supplied tokens.
The crash is due to an out-of-bounds copy or invalid pointer inside Decode_DER_ASN1_ST rather than an unrelated libc or allocator bug.
limitations: Ghidra MCP could not decompile Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, CERT_Load, or the JNI function due to server errors, so exact argument flows and bounds checks could not be inspected.
The precise nature of the memcpy misuse (source vs destination overflow, or null/invalid pointer) is unknown.
The exact application-level pathways that supply data to UbikeyJni.jGetOrg(byte[]) and their trust boundaries were not available, so remote exploitability is inferred rather than proven.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted DER/ASN.1 certificate blob to code that calls UbikeyJni.jGetOrg(byte[])
prerequisites: Attacker can influence the byte[] argument passed to UbikeyJni.jGetOrg (e.g., via remote certificate, file content, NFC token, or IPC).
The vulnerable libUbikeyJni.so version is present and used by the app in a context reachable from untrusted input.
Standard Android environment without hardening that would completely neutralize heap corruption arising from memcpy overflow.
exploit_pipeline: Identify an application feature that invokes UbikeyJni.jGetOrg(byte[]) with data derived from an external source (e.g., certificate import or remote authentication).
Construct malformed DER/ASN.1 data with inconsistent or oversized length fields to drive Decode_DER_ASN1_ST into performing an out-of-bounds memcpy during field extraction.
Deliver this crafted DER blob to the application via the chosen entrypoint so that it is wrapped into a byte[] and passed down to the JNI method.

Iterate on the payload while observing crashes (e.g., via logcat) to stabilize a reliable denial-of-service and, with deeper analysis of heap layout, explore potential controlled memory corruption primitives.
exploit.notes: Exact triggering component, package name, and activity are unknown; replace <PACKAGE>/<ACTIVITY> with the real values once the relevant feature that calls UbikeyJni.jGetOrg(byte[]) is identified.
